summary: A client complained that agile development caused difficulties too early in their project, but Fowler argues this early pain is actually one of agile's greatest benefits. The waterfall process defers problem discovery until late in projects when there's little time or energy to address them effectively. Iterative development intentionally flushes out problems early, giving teams more time to cope or the option to cancel before investing too much. Fowler recommends reflecting on past projects to identify how problems could have surfaced earlier.
key_points: Early difficulties in agile projects are a feature, not a bug—they represent problems being discovered when they can still be addressed
Waterfall's greatest flaw is deferring problem discovery until late in projects when options are limited
Iterative cycles intentionally surface problems early, providing time to either fix them or cancel before overinvesting
Teams should actively reflect on past projects to find ways to make problems appear earlier
money_quotes: This early pain is one of the great benefits of an agile or indeed any iterative development process.
Probably my greatest problem with it is how it tends to defer discovery of problems till late in the project, at which point there's little time or energy to deal with them effectively.
Iterative cycles try to flush out as many problems as possible as early as possible.
This gives you more time to cope, or at least raises the problems early enough to cancel before investing too much money and effort in a problematic project.
The more pain you get earlier, the better.
key_insight: Early pain in software projects is valuable because discovering problems sooner gives you more options to address them or cut your losses.
